text: We present a full video case series on our technique for the retroperitoneal lymph node dissection using the DaVinci XI platform.
Our case series is aimed to highlight that the robotic retroperitoneal lymph node dissection can be performed safely.

The authors believe that the robotic approach with 3D visualization and stability of the platform allow for safe and targeted dissection of retroperitoneal disease, including colonic adenocarcinoma and carcinoid tumors.
For brevity, we only show the steps of the retroperitoneal lymph node dissection in these cases.
We begin by hanging the transverse mesocolon to the anterior abdominal wall.
The DJ flexure is medialized.
This is to allow access to the retroperitoneum.
To begin the retroperitoneal dissection, we use an inferior mesenteric vein first approach, a familiar area for general and colorectal surgeons.
The dissection underneath the IMV ensures its preservation.
Here we can see the vessel sealer dissecting along the gonadal vein, taking the fatty tissue and the avid lymph nodes seen on the PET scan.
The gonadal vein forms a lateral boundary for the dissection.
We can see it draining to the left renal vein, which forms a superior boundary.
The anterior aorta forms the medial boundary.
The dissection continues along the relatively bloodless aortic plane, ensuring there is no lymphovascular tissue remaining anterior to the aorta.
The vessel sealer is useful because it can grasp, cold cut, and coagulate as a wristed instrument on the 3D platform.
We see here the full left para-aortic lymphovascular clearance within the labelled boundaries.
To optimize access to the retroperitoneum and aorta, the duodenum is elevated and the cut edge of the ileocolonic mesentery is suspended with an adopted Venetian blind technique.

This is performed with approximately six passes of 2-0 PDS sutures through the mesentery to the anterior abdominal wall, which allows for a broad front spread of the load and stops tearing of the tissue.
The robotic haemlocks are placed at each side.
The dissection begins along the right common iliac artery.
Given that this patient has had a previous right nephrectomy, there is no right ureter identified.
We may also encounter some more scar tissue.
The intent is to selectively clear the PET avid lymphatic tissue over the IVC, but also over the aorta.
Lymphovascular tissue is first cleared anterior to the aorta down to the inferior boundary, which is the right common iliac artery.
Here we can see the IVC on view next to the aorta.
Attention is focused now on the inferior mesenteric artery where lymphatic tissue is being taken off.
A small chioleic is encountered and it is mechanically occluded.
Here we can again define the left border of the dissection, which is the aorta and the inferior border, which is the right common iliac artery.
We then turn to clearing the lymphatic tissue off the inferior vena cava.
This is the location of the pet avid lesion.
We encounter small perforating vessels along the inferior vena cava.
These are clipped and ligated.
These collateral vessels may be the result of the patient's previous right nephrectomy.
We complete the clearance along the right common iliac artery.
Finally, we have on view the boundaries of the lymphatic dissection.
The aorta medially, the right common iliac inferiorly, the inferior vena cava cleared up to the duodenum with the ileal mesentery suspended above.
and the psoas muscle laterally.
Scoring of the peritoneum begins over the left common ilac artery.
The artery and ureter are identified and preserved.
The dissection is continued inferiorly towards the aortic bifurcation and extended laterally towards the IVC, which forms the lateral boundary of the dissection.
The 3D modelling seen in the inferior aspect of the video acted as a good reference point intraoperatively.
This modelling is currently undertaken in our department.
Here the malignant node has been identified.
We can see the tumor abutting the aorta with potential infiltration of the adventitia.
Although the patient has had a previous anterior resection, the inferior mesenteric artery is retaken flush to the aorta with hemlock clips and divided.
The 3D optics and stability of platform allows for precision of the dissection, which ensures the preservation of aorta and makes this surgery possible.
The robotic platform presents a 3D view to the surgeon as opposed to the 2D view we see here in the video.
We can see the interface between the aorta and the cicatrizing tumour.
There is a distinct tumour fibrous plane that allows for dissection of the plaque off the aorta.
We have cleared the aorta up to the bifurcation, the inferior vena cava,
and the inferior mesenteric artery, which is clipped high and out of view in this frame.
We illustrate a tailored left retroperitoneal lymph node clearance.
The duodenum has already been medialised and the mesocolum was sutured and left ureter identified and preserved.
Similar to the first case presented, we take an IMV first approach.
Dissection underneath the IMV ensures its preservation.
The left renal vein and the left gonadal vein are both identified.
The left renal vein forms the superior boundary of this dissection.
The aorta is also identified.
The aim is to clear the nodal tissue from the level of the aortic bifurcation up to the left renal vein, preserving the IMA.
We can see here the IMA has an oblique course coming from the aorta.
The key here is in preserving the IMA but making sure we have taken all the tissue overlying.
We turn now to the pathological area that was seen on the PET scan and complete the dissection.
There is enough taken to ensure it's a representative sample from the retroperitoneum.
The dissection has been completed.

We can see here the left psoas, the inferior mesenteric artery with its oblique takeoff, the inferior mesenteric vein, the renal vein, the aorta and the lymphatic tissue which has been left over the inferior vena cava.
This video has presented a new frontier in robotic retroperitoneal surgery, as it illustrates a pathology-selective approach to dissection.
We have demonstrated the advantages of the robotic platform, its range of use in retroperitoneal disease.
